coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and characterization of carbon quantum dots decorated hollow porous graphitic carbon nitride through polyaniline for photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Balakumar, Vellaichamy
Ramalingam, Manivannan
Sekar, Karthikeyan
Chuaicham, Chitiphon
Sasaki, Keiko
coredata.dc:description: Metal-free photocatalysts are widely used to decontaminate aqueous solutions by eliminating toxic and non-biodegradable compounds. It is desirable to develop a photocatalyst with high charge separation and migration efficiency. The addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to graphitic carbon nitride with polyaniline (PANI) can improve its light absorption abilities and reduce the recombination of holes and electrons. In this study, a novel CQDs decorated on PANI with hollow porous graphitic carbon nitride (CN) was fabricated via an in situ polymerization followed by an ultra-sonication. The optimal CQDs–loaded CN-PANI nanocomposite exhibited the high visible light absorption with a high specific surface area. Furthermore, better photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) was achieved under the visible light. The improved photocatalytic activity of CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) can be attributed to its higher charge separation, and destruction of recombination rate through the heterojunction of excited electrons among CN, PANI, and CQDs. This effect was further confirmed by high photocurrent intensity, low photoluminescence emission, and electrical resistance. In addition, different parameters including catalyst weight, initial CIP concentration, and interfering effect of anions during CIP removal were investigated. The main active species in the degradation of CIP were identified to h+, •OH, and •O2 − through the scavenger test. The high reusability and stability of the photocatalyst composite were also verified. The degradation intermediates and reaction pathways were identified. Furthermore, the effectiveness of the photocatalyst was evaluated using different toxic pollutants including imidacloprid, tetracycline, phenol, and rhodamine B under similar conditions. The CQDs–decorated CN-PANI was proved as a promising material for efficient photodegradation of toxic pollutants in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride
Polyaniline
Photocatalytic degradation
Ciprofloxacin
Toxic pollutants

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pulp densities on photocatalytic activity
Degradation efficiency of different pollutants by CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ultra-Microscopy Research Centre (URC) at Kyushu University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science London

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: V. B. and K. S. would like to thank the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) for providing a postdoctoral fellowship for foreign researchers (P19393) and the research grant (JP19F19393). HR(S)TEM performed at the Ultra-Microscopy Research Centre (URC) at Kyushu University. This work was partly supported by Nanotechnology Platform Program (Molecule and Material Synthesis) of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT), Japan.

V. B. and K. S. would like to thank the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) for providing a postdoctoral fellowship for foreign researchers (P19393) and the research grant (JP19F19393). HR(S)TEM performed at the Ultra-Microscopy Research Centre (URC) at Kyushu University. This work was partly supported by Nanotechnology Platform Program (Molecule and Material Synthesis) of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT), Japan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . (A) XRD patterns, (B) BET surface area, (C) UV-DRS spectra of all the synthesized samples and (D) FT-IR spectra of CN (curve a), CN-PANI (curve b), CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%) (curve c), CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) (curve d) and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%) (curve e).
TEM images of (A-B) CN and (C-D) CN-PANI and CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) nanocomposite with different magnifications (E-I).
(A) STEM image of CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) and the corresponding elemental mapping of (B) C, (C) N, and (D) O.
XPS spectra for CN, CN-PANI and CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) nanocomposite: (A) survey, (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s and (D) O1s regions.

(A) Photoluminescence spectra, (B) photocurrent spectra and (C) impedance spectra of all the synthesized samples. (D) effect of catalysts weight on the degradation of CIP, (E) effect of initial CIP concentration and (F) effect of interfering anions (100 mg/L) (a) Cl−, (b) NO3 −, (c) SO4 2−, (d) HCO3 − and (e) PO4 3− on the degradation of CIP (Catalyst CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%): 50 mg/50 ml; 10 mg/L CIP and visible light).

(A) Photocatalytic degradation of CIP using WC (without catalyst), WL (without light), CN, CN-PANI, CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%) (50 mg catalyst with 10 mg/L CIP under visible light), (B) different time interval UV–Vis spectra of CIP degradation using CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), (C) degradation efficiency of (1) CN, (2) CN-PANI, (3) CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), (4) CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), and (5) CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%) and (D) pseudo-first-order kinetics plots for CIP degradation.

UV–Vis spectra of the initial and reacted samples at different time intervals, (A) imidacloprid (B) tetracycline, (C) phenol and (D) rhodamine B using CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) nanocomposite (50 mg catalyst with each 10 mg/L pollutants concentration under the visible light).

(A) Photocatalytic CIP degradation efficiency of CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) during 5 cycles, with (B) XRD patterns of CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), (C) XPS C 1s spectra, and (D) N 1s spectra before and after 5 cycles.
The overall synthetic procedures for CN-PANI-CQDs nanocomposites.
Possible photocatalytic CIP degradation mechanism of CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) under visible light irradiation.
Proposed CIP degradation pathway using CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) under visible light irradiation.
. BET surface areas, pore volumes and band gap of all the synthesized samples.
Summary of photocatalytic degradation efficiency of previous works on g-C3N4 and its composites compared with the present CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%).

head.ce:title: Fabrication and characterization of carbon quantum dots decorated hollow porous graphitic carbon nitride through polyaniline for photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Earth Resources Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Kyushu University, 744 Motooka, Nishiku, Fukuoka 819-0395, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Earth Resources Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Kyushu University, 744 Motooka, Nishiku, Fukuoka 819-0395, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Advanced Organic Materials Engineering, Chungnam National University, 220 Gung-dong, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-764, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Advanced Organic Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Advanced Organic Materials Engineering, Chungnam National University, 220 Gung-dong, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-764, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science & Humanities, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science & Humanities, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports on the synthesis and characterization of an innovative CQDs–decorated porous g-C3N4 (CN) with PANI hybrid nanocomposite for the degradation of a host of toxic pollutants including ciprofloxacin in the environment.

Metal-free photocatalysts are widely used to decontaminate aqueous solutions by eliminating toxic and non-biodegradable compounds. It is desirable to develop a photocatalyst with high charge separation and migration efficiency. The addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to graphitic carbon nitride with polyaniline (PANI) can improve its light absorption abilities and reduce the recombination of holes and electrons. In this study, a novel CQDs decorated on PANI with hollow porous graphitic carbon nitride (CN) was fabricated via an in situ polymerization followed by an ultra-sonication. The optimal CQDs–loaded CN-PANI nanocomposite exhibited the high visible light absorption with a high specific surface area. Furthermore, better photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) was achieved under the visible light. The improved photocatalytic activity of CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) can be attributed to its higher charge separation, and destruction of recombination rate through the heterojunction of excited electrons among CN, PANI, and CQDs. This effect was further confirmed by high photocurrent intensity, low photoluminescence emission, and electrical resistance. In addition, different parameters including catalyst weight, initial CIP concentration, and interfering effect of anions during CIP removal were investigated. The main active species in the degradation of CIP were identified to h+, •OH, and •O2 − through the scavenger test. The high reusability and stability of the photocatalyst composite were also verified. The degradation intermediates and reaction pathways were identified. Furthermore, the effectiveness of the photocatalyst was evaluated using different toxic pollutants including imidacloprid, tetracycline, phenol, and rhodamine B under similar conditions. The CQDs–decorated CN-PANI was proved as a promising material for efficient photodegradation of toxic pollutants in water.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic H2 production, CO2 reduction, water splitting, and the degradation of hazardous pollutants in water using metal-free graphitic carbon nitride (CN) have been extensively attracted [1–6], because they are the environmental friendly and promising technology to contribute sustainable developing goals (SDGs). The CN is metal-free without causing the secondary pollutions, but has disadvantages to overcome such as fast recombination of charge carriers, low surface area, and visible light harvesting effect that hinder its application [7,8]. At present, different modifications of pure CN, including electronic structure, surface modifications via elemental doping, metal or metal oxide doping, making the defects and composite design have been studied to improve its application [9–18]. Recently, the hollow CN structure has gained attention because of its high specific surface area and improved visible light harvesting ability via multiple reflection of incident light within the interior voids when compared to bulk CN [19,20]. Hence, the construction of a porous visible‐light active CN structure is of vital importance. On the other hand, electron-conducting polymers (for example, polyaniline (PANI), polypyrrole, and polythiophene) composited with CN have been widely concerned, especially for photocatalytic applications [21–24]. Among them, PANI is the most intensively studied polymer with a delocalized π–π conjugated structure, which enables high visible light harvesting and excellent electron and hole transporting properties. In addition, it is a low-cost material that is easy to synthesize and it possesses good environmental stability and high conductivity [25,26]. For example, the PANI composite with CN photocatalysts synthesized via an in situ method showed to improve the photo-generated carrier separation and dye degradation caused by the synergistic effect [21]. Recently, Jiang and coworkers [27] synthesized a hydrogel 3D hierarchical nanostructure of PANI/CN via in situ polymerization and utilized it for photocatalytic removal of methylene blue (MB). Yu et al. [28] reported PANI/CN hybrid composites to promote the degradation of oxytetracycline and the evolution rate of H2. Therefore, a PANI composite with porous CN is possibly expected to efficiently improve photocatalytic activity.

In recent years, quantum dots (QDs) based photocatalytic composites have been proven to be an available strategy to enhance the photocatalytic charge separation and transfer efficiency [29–32]. Especially, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based photocatalysts have considerable attention in recent years because of their high electrical conductivity, good optical and catalytic properties [33–36]. Moreover, CQDs act as good electron mediators to reduce the recombination of electron and hole pairs and to improve the photocatalytic activity [37]. Nowadays, CN decorated with CQDs improves charge separation efficiency and restricts the recombination rate of electron and hole pairs [4]. Furthermore, CQDs can strongly interact with CN and provide more active sites for high H2 evolution and degradation of toxic chemicals [38–41]. Additionally, CQDs are rich in precursors and can be utilized for the construction and utilization of low-cost and metal-free CN based photocatalysts. For example, Jian et al. [42] constructed a CQDs/CN nanocomposite, which exhibited excellent electron transfer properties and degradation of MB. In addition, CQDs/CN photocatalysts were developed by Wang et al. in a hydrothermal method and they showed higher photocatalytic H2 production under the visible light [43]. Very recently, Zhao et al. [4] fabricated CQDs–modified tubular CN, which presented the excellent visible-light driven degradation of carbamazepine. In addition, CQDs decorated on PANI composites have been reported and utilized for various applications like electrochemical sensors [44] and supercapacitors [45,46].

However, both of CQDs and CN are negatively charged, so CQDs/CN composites seem to be not so stable. Herein, this is the first report of an innovative CQDs–decorated porous g-C3N4 (CN) with PANI hybrid nanocomposite prepared by in situ polymerization followed by an ultra-sonication method. The photocatalytic activity of the CN-PANI-CQDs nanocomposite was evaluated based on the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) containing wastewater under visible light. The optimal CQDs–loaded nanocomposite showed greatly improved photocatalytic activity compared to all other studied single, binary, and ternary composites. In addition, the effect of interference, catalyst weight, CIP concentration, and other organic, pharmaceutical, and pesticides containing wastewater degradation efficiencies were also investigated on the optimal composite. Furthermore, a scavenger test was conducted, and the detailed degradation mechanism and pathways were established.

Based on the method specified by Pan et al., [47], CQDs was prepared heating 2 g of citric acid in a crucible at 200 °C using the heating mantle, and then liquated. The color changed from yellow to orange in less than 30 min indicating the formation of CQDs. Then, the solution was dissolved in 100 ml of 1 M NaOH to neutralize. The prepared stock suspensions of CQDs was considered as 100%. Finally, CQDs were obtained for further studies. The presence of CQDs was confirmed by UV–Vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as shown in Fig. S1(A-D). The CQDs were characterized by absorption at 321 nm and emission at 426 nm. The average particle size of CQDs was observed around 6 nm through TEM.

The polymeric porous carbon nitride was fabricated using thermal polymerization. In a typical preparation, 5 g of urea was placed in a sealed crucible and calcined at 550 °C for 4 h in a muffle furnace with heating rate 10 °C/min. After calcination, the obtained yellow solid product was labeled as CN.

A 500 mg of synthesized porous CN was dispersed in 20 ml of distilled water and 0.2 ml of aniline was added to the solution. Then, 0.479 g of ammonium persulfate (APS) with the molar ratio of aniline: APS = 1:1.25 was dissolved in 0.01 M HCl, which was added drop-wise to the first solution. The solution became greenish indicating the formation of PANI. Then, the solution was stirred for another 6 h, and a dark green precipitate was formed in the solution. Finally, the precipitate was filtered using a Whatman filter paper and washed three times using ethanol and water and dried at 110 °C for 12 h. The sample was denoted as CN-PANI.

A 1.0 mg of synthesized CN-PANI was dispersed in distilled water. The as-synthesized CQDs solution, a certain volume (2.5, 5.0 and 7.5 ml of CQDs was considered as 2.5%, 5.0%, and 7.5%) were added to the above solutions. Then, the solution was ultra-sonicated for 20 min and dried at 60 °C overnight. The samples were denoted as CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%). The overall synthetic procedures are given in Scheme 1 .

The degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) was evaluated using all the synthesized CN composites, that is, CN, CN-PANI, CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%), under visible-light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation. First, 50 ml of 10 mg/L CIP was prepared, and 50 mg of the synthesized sample (pH = 6.12) was added and magnetically stirred in the dark condition for 20 min to reach the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. A 2 mL supernatant was taken and filtered using a 0.45-µm pore sized membrane filter (Cellulose) to measure the CIP concentration by the absorbance at 272 nm. Then, the remaining suspension was irradiated using a 500-W Xe lamp, and the supernatant was collected at different time intervals to determine the concentration of CIP. Similar procedures were adopted to degrade 10 mg/L imidacloprid, tetracycline, phenol, and rhodamine B at 269, 357, 270 and 533 nm, respectively. The concentration of CIP was detected by a high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), with a C18 column. A mixture of 20% 5 mM CH3COONa, 15% acetonitrile, and 65% 2% v/v acetic acid was used as the mobile phase with a flow rate of 1.0 ml min−1.

A 5.0 mg of each photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 µL of Nafion and 450 µL of ethanol and ultra-sonicated for 10 min. Then, 50 µL of this suspension was coated on the surface of the sheet-like platinum electrode, and the electrode was sintered at 60 °C for 30 min. The prepared electrode was used as a working electrode and Ag/AgCl (aqueous) was used as the reference and a Pt wire was used as the counter electrode for the photocurrent and impedance measurements. The reaction was carried out in 0.1 M NaCl as electrolyte under light irradiation of 500 W Xe lamp.

The crystalline phases of the photocatalysts were identified using X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) as shown in Fig. 1 A. CN exhibited a diffraction peak at 27.24° in 2θ, which indicates the interlayer-stacking of the 002 plane of its aromatic rings [48]. Compared XRD pattern of the CN-PANI composite with CN, the additional week diffraction peaks were observed at 19.97° and 25.0° in 2θ, which are assigned to the 020 and 200 reflections of the polymeric backbone introduced in the composite [21,28]. These results demonstrate that the introduction of PANI does not affect the crystal structure of CN phases. In addition, after the decoration of the CN-PANI composite surface with varying quantities (2.5 to 7.5%) of CQDs, there was no difference in the diffraction peaks of CN-PANI, but the intensities of the characteristic peaks of both CN and PANI decreased. This indicates the successful decoration of CQDs on the surface of the CN-PANI composite. Moreover, diffraction peaks of CQDs were not observed because of their relatively small quantities and low crystallinity.

The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface areas based on the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms are presented in Fig. 1B. All the samples exhibited type IV (mesoporous materials) adsorption–desorption isotherm loops according to the classification of the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC). Pure CN had a surface area of 45.6 cm2/g, which was similar to that of the type H3 hysteresis loop [47,48]. The CN-PANI composites exhibited a similar type of hysteresis loop and a specific surface area of 29.32 m2/g. In the CQDs–loaded composite with an optimal content of 5% of CQDs, the specific surface area increased and the particles were uniformly decorated. In the CQDs–loaded CN-PANI composite with 7.5% of CQDs, the surface area decreased because the particles were aggregated. The BET specific surface areas of the synthesized samples are summarized in Table 1 . These results are consistent with that of the SEM and TEM observation indicating high porosities and specific surface areas.

Differential reflectance spectroscopy was applied to investigate on the optical absorption properties of CN, CN-PANI, CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%), as shown in Fig. 1C. Pure CN has a visible light-adsorption edge at approximately 450 nm, which is caused by an n–π* transition (non-bonding orbitals to π* orbitals transition) that occurs within the triazine rings and the electronic charge transmission of N 2p orbitals to C 2p orbitals [1–5]. In comparison to pure CN, the light absorption capacities of CN-PANI composites were significantly enhanced up to the near infrared (NIR) region, which is due to the incorporation of PANI. The broad band at 600–800 nm corresponds to the bipolaron band transitions and the presence of −NH- was derived from PANI [21,26,28]. Significantly, because of the introduction of CQDs, the CN light adsorption edge was enhanced to beneficially improve the photocatalytic performance of the composite at a later stage. The optical band gap energy can be calculated through the Tauc equation (Eq. (1)) as summarized in Table 1.

The Fourier-transform infrared spectra of the pure CN, CN-PANI, CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%) are given in Fig. 1D. Pure CN exhibited characteristic bands at 1252, 1328, 1419, 1564 and 1640 cm−1, all of which correspond to the CN heterocycles (stretching vibration modes). The broad band that occurs from 3000 to 3500 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration modes of −NH and −OH, where the −OH is attributed to the presence of physically adsorbed H2O. Furthermore, the band at 809 cm−1 was attributed to the characteristic breathing mode of triazine units [48]. The spectrum of CN-PANI exhibited quinoid and benzenoid ring deformations of PANI bands at 1565 and 1473 cm−1. A new band at 1129 cm−1 due to C–H deformation and N–H stretching vibration modes of PANI [26,28] were observed. The CQDs at different concentrations did not cause changes in the characteristic CN bands but they decreased their intensities because of π–π stacking and hydrogen bonding [43].

The SEM images of CN, CN-PANI, CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%) were observed as shown in Fig. S2. Pure CN shows a layered structure. The surface of the CN-PANI composite showed some smoothness because of the decoration of PANI. After the incorporation of CQDs on the CN-PANI surface, the roughness of the surface increased compared to CN-PANI, thereby indicating successful decoration with CQDs. As the content of CQDs increased from 2.5% to 7.5%, surface roughness and particle size increased. These results clearly demonstrate that the CQDs influenced the surface morphologies of CN-PANI. These results were further confirmed by high resolution (HR) TEM. The TEM image in Fig. 2 (A and B) displays that the CN in a highly porous sheet and CN-PANI in a layered structure (Fig. 2 (C and D). These results indicate that the porous sheet of CN acts as a substrate for the incorporation of PANI. After the incorporation of CQDs on the CN-PANI composite, small particles (average size of 6 nm) were observed, confirming its successful decoration with CQDs (Fig. 2E–I). Finally, elemental mapping of the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) was conducted as shown in Fig. 3 . The C, N, and O are distributed in the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) nanocomposite. The uniform distribution of CQDs indicates the successful formation of the ternary CN-PANI - CQDs (5.0%) nanocomposite.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterized the binding energies of constituent elements in CN, CN-PANI, and CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) as shown in Fig. 4 A. The C 1s orbitals in the synthesized samples (Fig. 4B) were analyzed to detect the binding energies (E B) of 284.6, 287.6 and 289.8 eV that are ascribed to CC, NCN, and CO for CN, CN-PANI, and CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), respectively [46–48].

In the N 1s spectra (Fig. 4C), the E B of 399.6 eV and 401.1 eV can be attributed to the presence of N-(C)3 (tertiary nitrogen) and CNH groups of CN. The peaks at E B[N 1s] = 399.6 and 401.1 eV are typical of CNC and NH groups, which confirm the presence of graphitic carbon nitride with a graphite-like sp2-bonded structure and PANI [21,28]. The O 1s spectra of the samples exhibit two component peaks at approximately E B[O 1s] = 531.7 and 533.8 eV, corresponding to the CO bond and H2O, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4D [39]. Small shifts (~0.5 eV) in E B of the spectra for the CN-PANI - CQDs (5.0%) composite in C 1s, N 1s and O 1s suggest the successful decoration of the surface of CN-PANI with CQDs [5].

The synthesized composites were excited at 330 nm using photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) and the results are depicted in Fig. 5 A. A strong peak at 438 nm is ascribed to the n-π* transition of lone pair electrons over the N-atom of the CN [48,49]. For the CN-PANI, the PL intensity was reduced, and slight blue shifted compared to pure CN, which is attributed to the PANI in the composite. This indicates low recombination of charge carriers and low interaction between CN and PANI. In addition, with the incorporation and increase of concentrations of CQDs from 2.5% to 5.0%., the emission peak intensity significantly decreased. But, when the content of CQDs increased from 5.0% to 7.5%, the PL intensity increased when compared to the 5% CQDs–loaded composite because a higher content of CQDs acts as an electron sink and increases the rate of recombination. We further investigated the contribution of CQDs in CN-PANI toward charge separation and recombination using photocurrent (PC) and impedance measurements [50,51]. The introduction of visible-light illumination in CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) displays a higher photocurrent response compared to all other single, binary, and ternary composites for repeated on–off cycles (Fig. 5B). Even after five repeated on–off cycles the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) composite shows stable and higher photocurrent response. In addition, CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) composite shows a smaller radius in the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy derived Nyquist plot compared to all other composites (Fig. 5C). Both results indicate the enhanced charge separation and reduction in the recombination of CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), which is in good agreement with the PL spectra. These results clearly indicate that the optimal CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) composite might be better for photocatalytic activity at a later stage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pulp densities on photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) catalyst on the photocatalytic degradation of CIP was studied by varying its weight proportion between 30 and 100 mg/50 ml as shown in Fig. 5D. When the catalyst weight increases from 30 to 50 mg/50 ml, the degradation rate increases from 76.3% to 87.6%. On further increase of catalyst weight to 100 mg/50 ml, the CIP degradation efficiency was not altered significantly. These results indicate that the increase in active sites for the degradation of CIP occurs at 50 mg/50 ml; however, further increase of catalyst weight shielded the surface and restricted light irradiation. Similar studies have been reported by Nasseh et al. and Yan et al. [52,53]. Moreover, the higher weight proportion of catalysts induced the self-consumption of radicals due to the aggregation at the catalyst’s surface. The results show that the optimal pulp density was 50 mg/50 ml for the degradation of CIP.

Effect of the initial CIP concentration on the photodegradation activity was evaluated using the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) photocatalyst as shown in Fig. 5E. When the initial CIP concentration increases from 10 mg/L to 30 mg/L, the degradation efficiency of the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) photocatalyst decreases because of the availability of active sites, shielding of the catalyst surface by target pollutants, reduction of penetration of visible light, and production of numerous intermediates that compete with the main target [54,55]. All these factors hinder the degradation efficiency of the catalyst and the generation of reactive species. Hence, the CIP degradation experiments were carried out using a low initial concentration.

In general, contaminated water contains major inorganic anions including chloride (Cl−), nitrate (NO3 −), sulfate (SO4 2−), bicarbonate (HCO3 −), and phosphate (PO4 3−). The effect of the interfering anions (100 mg/L) on the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) was evaluated as shown in Fig. 5F. In the presence of Cl− and CO3 2−, only marginal changes in the degradation efficiencies were found because of the shielding of light and catalyst surface due to contamination [56]. On the other hand, in the presence of NO3 − and SO4 2− ions, no significant changes were observed. However, in the presence of HCO3 − and PO4 3− ions, the degradation efficiency of CIP was significantly affected because of the reaction with hydroxide radicals and holes to trap and produce HPO4 2− and H2PO4 −, respectively [57,58]. Thus, the presence of interfering anions in the aqueous solution hampers the photocatalytic mineralization of CIP.

The photocatalytic degradation performance of synthesized CN, CN-PANI, CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%) were evaluated using CIP as a model pollutant. The experiment in darkness demonstrates adsorption of CIP in the presence of different photocatalysts and low quantities of adsorption were observed (Fig. 6 A). Then, CIP degradation was carried out under visible light irradiation and the observed degradation rates and efficiencies. Under the optimal CQDs concentration (5.0%) loaded CN-PANI composite shows better photocatalytic degradation of CIP than all the other studied single, binary, and ternary composites because of its higher surface area and charge separation, which destroyed the recombination rate and synergistic effect of CN-PANI-CQDs, which was confirmed by BET, PL, PC, and impedance results [21,28]. The CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%) composites displayed lower CIP photocatalytic degradation efficiency. This occurs because a low content of CQDs is not sufficient to enhance degradation, and a high content leads to the aggregation of the CN-PANI composite and it acts as an electron sink to initiate the recombination [5]. Moreover, the CIP concentrations were measured using UV–Vis spectra at 272 nm by drawing samples at the initial stage and at different time intervals of degradation. After the adsorption experiments, it was observed that the peak intensity of the CIP decreased and no shift was observed during visible light irradiation (Fig. 6B). Additionally, a new peak was observed at 211 nm corresponding to the formation of degradation intermediate products. Moreover, the percentage of degradation of all the synthesized samples are shown in Fig. 6C. The reaction kinetics of the photocatalytic degradation of CIP was calculated using eq. (1) as follows:
(1)
- lnC /
C 0
= kt
where, k is the rate constant, and C0 and C are the concentrations of CIP at reaction times of 0s and t, respectively. The kinetic rate constants of pure CN, CN-PANI, CN-PANI-CQDs (2.5%), CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%), and CN-PANI-CQDs (7.5%) were 0.0320, 0.0616, 0.0958, 0.1137, and 0.1020 min−1, respectively (Fig. 6D). The photocatalytic degradation of CIP was compared with those reported in previous literatures (Table 2 ) [59–69]. Compared to all reported catalysts, the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) nanocomposite developed by us exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for CIP degradation. In addition, the degradation process was again confirmed by HPLC and the results are shown in Fig. S3. The degradation percentage was observed at 94.7 % from HPLC. This results are well supported to the UV–Vis spectra results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation efficiency of different pollutants by CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%)
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of photocatalytic degradation of different contaminants, pesticides (imidacloprid), pharmaceuticals (tetracycline), organic compounds (phenol), and dyes (rhodamine B) using the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) system was evaluated (Fig. 7 A–D). In the dark condition, only a small quantity of imidacloprid adsorption by CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) occurred; however, under the visible light the degradation was higher than 80.1% within 70 min. The degradation process was monitored at different time intervals as shown in Fig. 7A. On the other hand, (Fig. 7 B–D) the degradation of tetracycline, phenol, and rhodamine B were 93.2%, 45.6%, and 97.9%, respectively. The rate constants of degradation by CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) were in the order of rhodamine B (0.3018 min−1) > imidacloprid (0.1261 min−1) > ciprofloxacin (0.1137 min−1) > tetracycline (0.0974 min−1) > phenol (0.0356 min−1). These results illustrate that the optimal CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) system can effectively degrade various toxic pollutants in the aqueous environment.

The effect of active species on the photodegradation of CIP was investigated through trapping experiments. In this study, the CIP photodegradation was evaluated using ethylenediaminetetracetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) to quench h+, •OH, and •O2 −, respectively [5]. When the hole scavenger EDTA-2Na or •OH scavenger IPA was added, the photodegradation efficiency of CIP decreased from 87.6% to 40.5% and 28.2%, respectively, indicating that the h+ and •OH play an un-ignorable role in the degradation process. On the other hand, the degradation efficiency for the BQ was 22.9% indicating that this scavenger has the predominant role in CIP degradation. These results indicate that •O2 −, h+, and •OH radicals are vital active species (Fig. S4A). Based on these results, the electron and hole transfer pathways of the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) system in the degradation of CIP and other toxic pollutants was tested under visible-light, as shown in Scheme 2 . From the XPS results (Fig. S4B and C), the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of the porous CN were calculated to be + 1.63 and −1.15 eV. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) potentials of PANI are at + 0.62 eV and −2.14 eV, respectively [21,49]. The band gap of PANI is 2.76 eV. Under visible-light, CN and PANI adsorb light and generate electrons and holes. Then, the generated electrons are moved from the valence band (VB) of CN to the conduction band (CB) of CN and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of PANI to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of PANI through π–π* transitions [21,26,28]. Both PANI and CN of LUMO and CB (−2.14 eV and −1.15 eV) was more negative than E0 (O2/•O2 − −0.036 eV) [70]. Therefore, the excited LUMO of PANI electrons are readily injected into the CB of CN and the electrons are directly reacting with oxygen to yield active species of superoxide radicals via CQDs. Moreover, the LUMO of PANI electrons are also directly reacting with oxygen to yield active species of superoxide radicals via CQDs. CQDs act as electron mediators to accept and transfer electrons, which leads to the reduction of the recombination of charge carriers [71,72]. In addition, the obtained •O2 − radical could reacted with H+ to form H2O2 and reacted with e- to produce •OH. The holes on the CN cannot generate the •OH radicals due to the lower VB potential than the potential of radical •OH/OH− (1.99 eV) [73]. On the other hand, the holes in the VB of directly react with target pollutants to degrade them. Moreover, the generated h+, •OH, and •O2 − are vital active radical species for the degradation of CIP and other target pollutants. These •OH, and •O2 − are further confirmed by ESR radical trapping measurement under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S5A and B, DMPO-•O2 − has a strong signal and DMPO-•OH was observed four-line spectrum (1:2:2:1) under visible light irradiation [70,73]. The degradation mechanistic pathway over the CN-PANI-CQDs (%) could be described the following reactions.
(2) CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) + hʋ → (e-) + (h+)
(3) CN (e-) & PANI (e-) + O2 → •O2 −
(4)
•O2 − + H+ → •OOH
(5)
•OOH + H+ + e− → H2O2
(6) H2O2 + e−→ •OH + OH−
(7)
•O2 − + CIP → Degradation products
(8)
•OH + CIP → Degradation products
(9) CN & PANI (h+) + CIP → Degradation products

The toxicity of degraded products that are formed by the photodegradation of toxic pollutants in water is an essential parameter. Here, the degraded products were analyzed using liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC–MS). As shown in Fig. S6, the possible CIP degradation intermediates and pathways (Scheme 3 ) were identified via four major degradation pathways. The degradation intermediates were detected during the process at: 355.21, 361.08, 377.06, 301.07, 289.08, 279.09, 273.16, 263.13, 256.96, 245.08, 229.14, 217.07, 189.04, 187.08, 173.06, 158.99, 143.01, 115.02, 106.08 and 100.00 m/z were observed which are the products of degrade quinolone moiety and removal of piperazine ring, and followed by the elimination of defluorination, decarboxylation, and decarbonylation [74,75,76]. It is known that pKa1 and pKa2 of CIP are 6.09 and 8.74, respectively which means that CIP is positively charged (CIP+) at pH less than 6.09, zwitterionic CIP (CIP±) at pH ~ 6.09–8.74 and anionic form (CIP-) at pH > 8.74 [70]. The defluorination could occur thermodynamically for all the three dissociation forms of CIP, but the most favorable for zwitterionic form [77]. In addition, all other degradation pathways (piperazine ring oxidation, hydroxylation in quinolone ring, decarboxylation, and decarbonylation) are most favorable pH at 6.0 [69,70]. Finally, the intermediate products were converted into small non-toxic molecules like CO2, H2O, F-, and other small mineralized molecules. Furthermore, the TOC of the CIP degraded solution over CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) composite was examined. In Fig. S7, the TOC removal efficiency attains around 75.1%, indicating that the CIP not only degrades and also mineralizes. However, TOC removal efficacy was 12.7% lower than the degradation efficiency owing to the existence of minor aliphatic residual compounds.

The reusability of the photocatalyst was tested in terms of the degradation of CIP in five consecutive recycling experiments as shown in Fig. 8 A. Results show that the optimal CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) composite displays stable photocatalytic activity even after repeating five cycles without any significant loss. The stability of the photocatalyst was confirmed by characterization using XRD and XPS. The XRD patterns of samples of both freshly prepared and after five photocatalytic cycles were almost similar (Fig. 8B) suggesting that the crystal structure of the photocatalyst was not affected even after long-term use. Moreover, the E B[C 1s] (Fig. 8C) and E B [N 1s] in XPS (Fig. 8D) did not change after photocatalytic reaction. The relative intensities in C 1s were slightly influenced by the contamination peak from the apparatus, not essential. The above results show that the CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) composite is a chemically stable and effective photocatalyst for the degradation of toxic pollutants in water and other environments.

A novel fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated hollow porous graphitic carbon nitride (CN) through polyaniline (PANI) was proposed via an in situ polymerization followed by an ultra-sonication. An optimal CN-PANI-CQDs (5.0%) nanocomposite showed the outstanding photocatalytic CIP degradation. Visible light absorption, high specific surface area, photocurrent intensity, lower photoluminescence emission, and electrical resistance leads to the high photocatalytic performance of the catalyst under visible light. In addition, the synergistic effect of CN, PANI, and CQDs also influenced the enhanced photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic activity of CN-PANI-CQDs nanocomposite was demonstrated for not only CIP but also imidacloprid, tetracycline, phenol, and rhodamine B under visible light. The photodegradation mechanism was elucidated through the active radical species trapping test. Even after five repeated cycles, the photocatalyst maintained the excellent reusability and stability. The order of degradation of photocatalytic pollutants is as follows; rhodamine B (0.3018 min−1) > imidacloprid (0.1261 min−1) > ciprofloxacin (0.1137 min−1) > tetracycline (0.0974 min−1) > phenol (0.0356 min−1). The synthesized ternary metal free visible light active photocatalyst is a promising material for a wide range of application for the water remediation without causing the secondary contaminations.

Vellaichamy Balakumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation and interpretation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Manivannan Ramalingam: Formal analysis. Karthikeyan Sekar: Formal analysis. Chitiphon Chuaicham: Formal analysis. Keiko Sasaki: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: V. B. and K. S. would like to thank the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) for providing a postdoctoral fellowship for foreign researchers (P19393) and the research grant (JP19F19393). HR(S)TEM performed at the Ultra-Microscopy Research Centre (URC) at Kyushu University. This work was partly supported by Nanotechnology Platform Program (Molecule and Material Synthesis) of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT), Japan.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.131739.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
